coredata.dc:title: Fe(III)-functionalized carbon dots—Highly efficient photoluminescence redox catalyst for hydrogenations of olefins and decomposition of hydrogen peroxide
coredata.dc:creator: Bourlinos, Athanasios B.
Rathi, Anuj K.
Gawande, Manoj B.
Hola, Katerina
Goswami, Anandarup
Kalytchuk, Sergii
Karakassides, Michael A.
Kouloumpis, Antonios
Gournis, Dimitrios
Deligiannakis, Yannis
Giannelis, Emmanuel P.
Zboril, Radek
coredata.dc:description: We present the first bottom-up approach to synthesize Fe(III)-functionalized carbon dots (CDs) from molecular precursors without the need of conventional thermal or microwave treatment and additional reagents. Specifically, sonication of xylene in the presence of anhydrous FeCl3 results in oxidative coupling of the aromatic substrate towards Fe(III)-functionalized CDs. The as-prepared CDs are spherical in shape with a size of 3–8nm, highly dispersible in organic solvents and display wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL). The iron ions attached to the surface endow the CDs with superior catalytic activity for olefin hydrogenation with excellent conversion and selectivity (up to 100%). The Fe(III)-CDs are more effective in the hydrogenation of a series of electron donating or withdrawing olefin substrates compared to conventional homogeneous or heterogeneous Fe(III)-based catalysts. The as-prepared heterogeneous nanocatalyst can be used repeatedly without any loss of catalytic activity. Importantly, the stability of the new catalysts can be easily monitored by PL intensity or quantum yield measurements, which certainly opens the doors for real time monitoring in a range of applications. Additionally, to the best of our knowledge, for the first time, the oxidative property of Fe-CDs was also explored in decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in water with the first order rate constant of 0.7×10−2 min−1, proving the versatile catalytic properties of such hybrid systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxidative coupling
Fluorescence
Graphene quantum dots
Redox nanocatalysis
Olefins reduction
Environmental remediation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sonication of xylene (mixture of ortho-, para-, and meta-form) in the presence of anhydrous FeCl3 results in polyaromatic networks. Stacking of these layers leads to the formation of fluorescent CDs under mild conditions.

Material characterization of Fe-CDs: PL excitation–emission map (a); the image of the sample under UV and day light (b); FT-IR (c); TGA/DTA analysis under air (d); high-resolution C 1s and survey (inset) XPS spectrum (e); high-resolution O 1s XPS spectrum (f).

Microscopic and DLS characterization of the Fe-CDs: AFM image and height profile (a); TEM image including chemical mapping of oxygen (red) and iron (green) on single dot in inset (b); DLS size distribution of Fe-CDs in acetone (c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article).

Schematic representation of catalytic application of Fe-CDs and its recyclability (three times) with the same conversion rate for reduction of styrene (a); comparison of ethyl benzene formation with Fe-CDs catalyst compared to blanc/control reaction (without catalyst) (b); various olefins that have been used for testing of catalytic activity of the Fe-CDs; the reduced bond is highlighted by pink color, the conversion rate is given as determined by GC using dodecane as internal standard, the selectivity was 100% in all cases except for 4-fluorostyrene. Reaction conditions: alkene (0.1mmol), EtOH (0.6mL), catalyst (20mg), N2H4 (30μL), RT, 24h (c). (d) 1st order linear fit for the decomposition of H2O2 using Fe-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article).

head.ce:title: Fe(III)-functionalized carbon dots—Highly efficient photoluminescence redox catalyst for hydrogenations of olefins and decomposition of hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science and Engineering, Cornell University, 326 Bard Hall, Ithaca, NY 14853, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Materials Science and Engineering, Cornell University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we present first bottom-up approach to synthesize Fe(III)-functionalized carbon dots from molecular precursors without need of conventional thermal or microwave treatment and additional reagents. As-prepared heterogeneous nanocatalyst can be used repeatedly without any loss of catalytic activity. Stability of new catalysts is monitored by photoluminescence intensity measurements, opening doors for real time monitoring in range of applications.

We present the first bottom-up approach to synthesize Fe(III)-functionalized carbon dots (CDs) from molecular precursors without the need of conventional thermal or microwave treatment and additional reagents. Specifically, sonication of xylene in the presence of anhydrous FeCl3 results in oxidative coupling of the aromatic substrate towards Fe(III)-functionalized CDs. The as-prepared CDs are spherical in shape with a size of 3–8nm, highly dispersible in organic solvents and display wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL). The iron ions attached to the surface endow the CDs with superior catalytic activity for olefin hydrogenation with excellent conversion and selectivity (up to 100%). The Fe(III)-CDs are more effective in the hydrogenation of a series of electron donating or withdrawing olefin substrates compared to conventional homogeneous or heterogeneous Fe(III)-based catalysts. The as-prepared heterogeneous nanocatalyst can be used repeatedly without any loss of catalytic activity. Importantly, the stability of the new catalysts can be easily monitored by PL intensity or quantum yield measurements, which certainly opens the doors for real time monitoring in a range of applications. Additionally, to the best of our knowledge, for the first time, the oxidative property of Fe-CDs was also explored in decomposition of hydrogen peroxide in water with the first order rate constant of 0.7×10−2 min−1, proving the versatile catalytic properties of such hybrid systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) represent one of the newest members of carbon-based materials with a wide spectrum of potential applications [1–3]. One advantage is their high biocompatibility in comparison with traditional semiconductor quantum dots. Thanks to this property, they have emerged as an attractive alternative for biomedical applications [4–8]. Their electroluminescence performance has made them competitive even for optoelectronic applications [9]. Numerous methods have been already proposed for the synthesis of these new materials, which can be categorized into two main types: top-down and bottom-up. In the former case, laser ablation or acid treatment of bulk carbon materials such as graphite, coal, and soot are the dominant approaches [10–12]. The latter category is based on so-called molecular synthetic approaches, where heat or microwaves are used to form a carbonaceous network from different molecular precursors [13–17]. This strategy enables tailoring the surface functionality, solubility, heteroatom content, and size of the dots by appropriate selection of the precursor. A new class of carboxylic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for DNA detection [18] and synthesis of fluorescent graphene quantum dots via simultaneous oxidation and cage-opening of fullerene (C60) [19] were reported by Pumera and co-workers.

Recently the direct synthesis of carbon dots by photochemical stitching of small aromatic molecules has been reported [20]. The new method represents a departure from conventional molecular synthetic protocols based on thermal or microwave treatment, and instead uses organic coupling reactions aided by light. No doubt, such organic coupling reactions can be quite attractive in terms of rational chemical design from the bottom-up. Nevertheless, they remain still largely unexplored.

Oxidative coupling of aromatic compounds by anhydrous FeCl3 provides a versatile and simple tool for the synthesis of complex polyaromatic networks [21,22]. Being a mild oxidizing agent, iron(III) chloride removes H atoms from the aromatic substrate leading to new carbon-carbon bonds between adjacent rings (i.e., forming a condensed network).

The use of carbon materials such as graphite, graphite oxide or carbon nanotubes as catalysts or catalyst supports in organic or inorganic synthesis is well established [23,24]. Carbon-based catalysts/supports are attractive due to low cost, toxicity, and density, high surface area, diverse surface functionality, sp2 basal planes and moderate insertion of heteroatoms (e.g., oxygen after oxidation) [25]. However, up to now little attention has been paid to the direct use of CDs in catalysis except for their application as solid supports for immobilization of catalytically active nanoparticles or as enhancers of photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical processes [26–30].

Herein, we report Fe-bearing CDs synthesized directly in one pot via oxidative coupling of xylene molecules by anhydrous FeCl3 and demonstrate their use as redox nanocatalysts for efficient olefins hydrogenation at room temperature and hydrogen peroxide decomposition with a rate constant comparable with the most efficient iron oxide based systems.

For the synthesis of the iron-containing carbon dots (Fe-CDs), 100mg anhydrous FeCl3 was suspended in 50mL xylene (98.5%, mixture of three isomers+15% ethylbenzene, AnalaR NORMAPUR, VWR Chemicals, BDH Prolabo) in glass vials with a screw cap (total 8 vials). The mixture in the vials after sonication for 20–24h in a sonication bath (130W, 45°C) resulted in a deep brown suspension. The suspension was centrifuged at 5000rpm for 20min in order to remove excess FeCl3 and the solvent was evaporated until dry by mild heating. The solid residue was rinsed several times with hexane and then dissolved in acetone. The acetone dispersion was filtered off in order to remove undissolved particles and the filtrate was evaporated until dry at room temperature to give Fe-CDs as a dark brown solid (yield: 15–30mg). The material was characterized by instruments listed in Supplementary material.

Substituted styrenes (>98%) and aqueous hydrazine hydrate (65%) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. EtOH was purchased from VWR and used without any further purification. In a 10mL round bottom flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer, styrene (0.10mmol) was dissolved in EtOH (0.6mL) and the Fe-CDs catalyst (20mg) was added followed by addition of aqueous hydrazine hydrate (30μL). The resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature for various times. The progress of the reaction was monitored by GC by taking aliquots from the reaction mixture at various time intervals. A control test without the use of catalyst was also conducted.

In a typical experiment, to a 10mL of ∼0.02M of peroxide solution, 10mg of Fe-CD sample was added. The dispersion was shaken at room temperature. At different time intervals, 1mL of the solution was taken from it and 9mL of 0.02M H2SO4 was added to the 1mL solution. The concentration of H2O2 at different time interval was calculated using pre-standardized permanganate solution using the equation:
2MnO4 − +5H2O2 +6H+ →2Mn+2 +5O2 +8H2O
Two additional experiments were performed using two reference solutions (one with just Fe-CDs and one with just H2O2) to obtain information about the backgrounds reactions.

X-Ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker Advance D8 diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. Transmission electron microscope images were obtained with a JEOL 2010 with LaB6 type emission gun, operating at 160kV. Elemental mapping was obtained using a HRTEM TITAN 60–300kV, operating at 300kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained in tapping mode with a 3D Multimode Nanoscope, using Tap-300G silicon cantilevers with a tip radius <10nm and a force constant of 20–75Nm−1. Samples were deposited onto silicon wafers (P/Bor, single side polished) from acetone solutions by drop casting. Raman spectra was recorded with a RM 1000 Renishaw micro-Raman system using a laser excitation line at 532nm (Nd:YAG) in the range 500–2000cm−1. FT-IR spectra of powder samples dispersed in KBr pellets were measured with a PerkinElmer GX, FT-IR spectrometer. The sample was first dried in vacuum at 60°C in order to remove any acetone traces. Thermal gravimetric (TGA)/differential thermal analysis (DTA) were performed in air at a rate of 5°Cmin−1 using a PerkinElmer Pyris Diamond TG/DTA. The dried sample was also redissolved in CDCl3 and 1H NMR analysis was performed using a JEOL spectrometer, JNM-ECA 600. XPS analysis was carried out on a PHI VersaProbe II (Physical Electronics) spectrometer using Al Kα source (15kV, 50W). Data was evaluated using the MultiPak (Ulvac-PHI, Inc) software. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments) equipped with a photomultiplier in a thermoelectrically cooled housing (R928P, Hamamatsu). The PL quantum yields (QY) were determined by an absolute method using the same fluorescence spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere with its inner surface coated with BENFLEC (Edinburgh Instruments). Spectral correction curves that take account of the spectral sensitivity of the monochromator, detector, sphere coating, and optics were provided by Edinburgh Instruments. The accuracy of the integrating sphere apparatus was tested against reference dyes with known QY: for Rhodamine 100 in ethanol (reference QY of 100%) and 2-Aminopyridine (reference QY of 84%).

In this paper, we report for the first time the direct use of metal-functionalized CDs as redox heterogeneous hybrid nanosystems. Based on the hypothesis of oxidative coupling, followed by attachment of Fe-species for the synthetic steps, we envisioned that the hybrid catalytic system should have the ability to modulate its redox property depending on the reaction condition and therefore could be utilized for both oxidation and reduction reaction. With that goal, we utilized the metal-functionalized carbon dots (Fe-CDs) for olefin reduction and also for hydrogen peroxide decomposition.

Olefin hydrogenation reactions are important industrial processes typically requiring either a homogeneous [31] or heterogeneous [32] catalyst. The reaction can be carried out under various reaction conditions using precious and semiprecious transition metal complexes [33]. However, homogeneous catalysts based on metal complexes suffer from poor functional group tolerance, low selectivity, high cost, and complicated catalyst preparation. In contrast, heterogeneous catalysts have gained significant acceptance due to the large range of inexpensive systems that can be used. Specifically, iron and iron-based catalysts offer several advantages over more conventional noble metal catalysts due to their low cost and availability [34,35].

Despite a sharp growth in the development of the nanocatalytic systems for the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide, there is still room for improvement especially in terms of their activity and/or selectivity of the process [36–38]. Under this scenario, hybrid nanosystems, derived from simple cheap precursors using simple synthetic techniques, which can be utilized for the decomposition of peroxide, are always welcome. Additionally, the possible orthogonal applications tremendously help to develop further multifunctional hybrid nanomaterials as exemplified in the current manuscript where in addition to olefin reduction, our Fe-doped carbon dots can be successfully utilized for the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide.

The synthesis of the iron-containing carbon dots Fe-CDs is carried out under sonication, i.e., in the absence of conventional thermal or microwave treatment (Fig. 1 ). Iron(III) chloride causes dehydrogenation of the aromatic substrate thus resulting in new C–C bonds and the formation of condensed polyaromatic sheets. Stacking of these sheets leads to the formation of CDs that contain iron(III) in the structure. We observed that sonication is important in order to speed up the formation of the dots. Moreover, xylene (with two methyl groups attached to the aromatic ring) is more reactive than benzene and toluene. This trend in reactivity is expected based on the mechanism of such reactions, which presumably involves the formation of arenium ions as intermediates [39]. The used xylene is a mixture of meta-, ortho-, and para-isomers and ethylbenzene. Accordingly, the reaction is influenced by the mixture composition, which in turn depends on the source. Interestingly, when instead of a mixture of pure meta-, ortho- or para-isomers are used no photoluminescent CDs are obtained.

Partial dehydrogenation of the substrate was evidenced by 1H NMR (Fig. S1 in Supplementary material). The sample with Fe-CDs shows dramatically lower intensity of methyl protons in comparison with xylene, which exhibits a ratio of aromatic (7.4ppm) to methyl protons (2.6ppm) of 2:3. The NMR data is consistent with the methyl groups being partly incorporated in the aromatic structure through dehydrogenation, with aromatic protons remaining on the edges (see inset in Fig. S1 in Supplementary material).

The CDs are highly dispersible in common organic solvents such as acetone, chloroform, and THF. The acetone dispersion emits green/yellow light as can be seen from PL excitation–emission map and also under a UV irradiation lamp (Fig. 2a and b). The overall emission maximum is around 530nm, when excited at 400nm and it shifts towards the red with increasing excitation wavelength. The excitation-dependent emission is very typical of CD systems. Although the reason for the dependence is a topic of a continued scientific discussion, it is generally accepted that the observed optical properties result from system heterogeneity [40], embedded emissive sp2 cluster-size distribution [41], or various emissive surface functionalities [42,43]. The absolute QY of the iron-containing dots is about 1% in dilute acetone (0.1mgmL−1). The reason for the much lower QY compared to other CDs might be due to quenching from the iron ions.

The IR spectrum in Fig. 2c shows typical absorptions at 1615cm−1 and 1380cm−1 (CC stretching of aromatic rings), 1100cm−1 and 1715cm−1 (C–O and CO, respectively), 2925cm−1 (methyl groups) and 578cm−1. The latter absorption is assigned to sp2 graphitic carbon and/or Fe–O bonds [44]. The Raman spectrum shows broad peaks of G (1583cm−1, sp2-hybridized) and D (1340cm−1, sp3-hybridized) bands typical for amorphous carbon nanomaterial (Fig. S2 in Supplementary material). The XRD pattern of the dots using a background-free holder (Si wafer) shows a very broad reflection centered at d 002 =4.6Å, corresponding to highly disordered carbon [45] (Fig. S3 in Supplementary material).

According to TGA, the decomposition of the dots commences near 300°C in air (Fig. 2d). Decomposition proceeds in two steps and it is accompanied by two exothermic peaks in DTA: one at 300°C due to methyl-group oxidation [46] and another at 350°C due to carbon combustion [47]. The residue after complete combustion of the carbon matrix is due to some form of iron oxide.

The C 1s and O 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of Fe-CDs are shown in Fig. 2e and f. The C 1s peak consists of two main components that can be assigned to sp2 (CC) and sp3 (C–C) carbons. Small peaks located in the high-energy tail of C 1s belong to carbon–oxygen groups. The presence of oxygen in the sample is probably caused by oxidation of the sample during processing. With respect to O 1s high-resolution spectrum, the asymmetric peak is typical for oxygen atoms in carboxyl and epoxy/hydroxyl groups. The shoulder in the low-energy tail of the spectrum could be assigned to iron ion (Fe–O–C) covalently bonded to the carbon matrix via carboxyl group [48]. The calculated XPS elemental atomic composition was 70.5% C, 22.3% O, and 7.2% Fe (survey spectrum in inset of Fig. 2e). The iron content is in fair agreement with ICPMS analysis (ca. 7wt.% Fe) and TG analysis discussed above. Magnetization and Mössbauer measurements (Fig. S4 in Supplementary material) show the iron species to be paramagnetic even at very low temperatures – a behavior typical for ionic Fe(III) (i.e., a temperature evolution of the mass magnetic susceptibility following the Curie law and a doublet typical for paramagnetic Fe3+ in the high spin state, respectively; see Fig. S4b in Supplementary material). The oxidation state of iron was also confirmed by high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. S5 in Supplementary material) where the main dominant peak was located at 711eV.

The size and morphology of the dots were studied by AFM and HR-TEM microscopy. Relatively uniform particles with spherical shape and void of any aggregation can be seen on the AFM image in Fig. 3a. The height of these particles is between 3 and 8nm. HR-TEM also confirmed the presence of ultrafine dots with sizes 3–5nm (Figs. 3b and S6 in Supplementary material). The corresponding chemical mapping analysis also revealed that iron as well as oxygen are homogeneously distributed on the Fe-CDs. Lastly, DLS measurements in acetone gave an average dot size of 6nm (Fig. 3c), which is in good agreement with the microscopy results.

The main emphasis of the work was on evaluation of the applicability of the Fe-CDs as catalysts in the hydrogenation of alkenes. Before testing with various alkenes, we focused on styrene as a model substrate. Reactions were run in ethanol at room temperature (Fig. 4 a,b and Table S1 in Supplementary material). Control reactions in the absence of catalyst were also performed for comparison. In addition, no conversion was observed in the absence of hydrazine hydrate (Table S1 in Supplementary material, entry 6).

Optimized reaction conditions were identified by conducting a series of experiments with different Fe-CDs catalyst loading (Table S1 in Supplementary material). Conversion increases with time. The highest conversion (98%) could be achieved after 24h at room temperature with 20mg of Fe-CDs catalyst using hydrazine hydrate as hydrogen source, while significantly lower loading of catalyst (10mg) showed still a very high (94%) conversion, if higher amount of hydrazine hydrate was used (Table S1 in Supplementary material, entry 1). In contrast, only 18% conversion is observed in the absence of the Fe-CD catalysts.

Increasing the catalyst amount (25mg) or addition of aqueous ammonia did not affect the conversion after 24h (Table S1 in Supplementary material, entries 3 and 5). Furthermore, we have investigated the influence of other reducing agents such as formic acid, sodium borohydride, and hydrazine monohydrate (Table S1 in Supplementary material, entries 7 and 8). Among various hydrogen sources investigated only hydrazine hydrate exhibited good catalytic activity (Table S1 in Supplementary material, entries 5, 7, and 8). It is also important to note that other iron(III)-based species including FeCl3 and γ-Fe2O3, as typical representatives of either homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts, led to low conversions of 42% and 17%, respectively (Table S1 in Supplementary material, entries 9 and 10), when the same amount of iron in the catalyst was used. This trend confirms the synergistic catalytic effects of Fe and C in the dots. To rule out the role of bare carbon dots in the catalytic activity, an experiment was run with carbon dots void of any iron (the iron was removed by washing in HNO3). Again the conversion was very low (18%, Table S1 in Supplementary material, entry 11). The enhanced catalytic efficacy of the Fe-CDs is probably caused by the uniform distribution of the iron on the surface without any distinct iron or iron oxide nanoparticles being present.

After optimizing the reaction conditions, the efficacy of the catalytic activity of the Fe-CDs was studied for various styrene substrates (Fig. 4c). In most cases, excellent conversions to the corresponding alkanes were obtained (94–98%) with great selectivity of terminal alkenes (100% in all cases except for 4-fluorostyrene with 90% selectivity). The conversion to ethyl benzene, 4-methoxy ethyl benzene, 3-nitro ethyl benzene, 4-fluoro ethyl benzene, and 4-chloro ethyl benzene was better than 98%, while 3-amino ethyl benzene and 4-methyl ethyl benzene were obtained in ∼95% conversion. It was noticed that 8% de-halogenated product was formed in the reduction of 4-flurostyrene. Furthermore, in the case of cyclooctene the reduced product was obtained in 93% conversion. Additionally, methyl cinnamate underwent reduction of CC bond and formed methyl 3-phenylpropanoate with 81% conversion under optimized reaction conditions, while β-methyl styrene showed a 38% conversion, which is far greater than previously reported results [29]. It was noticed that there is no further increase in β-methyl styrene conversion at longer times or by heating at 60°C. Moreover, phenyl acetylene was reduced with 93% conversion after 100h at room temperature into 59% ethyl benzene and 34% styrene, respectively (Scheme S1 in Supplementary material), which is very encouraging in pursuing hydrogenation of alkynes under such mild conditions to get the respective olefins and alkanes.

Next, the recyclability of the catalyst was studied. After each cycle, the catalyst was separated by centrifugation, washed with ethanol, and dried at 50°C under vacuum to remove residual solvent. A conversion of 98% was observed after three cycles, which suggests that there is no degradation of the catalyst. Since the catalyst is fluorescent, in addition to XPS analysis, the PL spectra were followed after each cycle. Fig. S7 in Supplementary material shows that the PL spectra, QY, and composition were virtually unchanged after each catalytic cycle. Decomposition of the catalyst by leaching of iron into the solution can be readily followed by studying their XPS patterns and intensity of fluorescence (Figs. S8 and S9 in Supplementary material) while the QY of the control CDs void of iron (prepared by treatment in nitric acid) is 8.3% QY vs. 1% of the original sample.

In order to explore the versatility of the Fe-based carbon dots, we investigated their potential in decomposition of hydrogen peroxide. For the determination of the concentration of peroxide present in the solution, the solution was titrated with pre-standardized MnO4 – solution (please see Supplementary material for details). As compared to its blank counterpart where no Fe-CDs have been used, the addition of Fe-CDs increased the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide drastically (Fig. S10 in Supplementary material). The rate constant of hydrogen peroxide decomposition achieved with Fe-CD nanocatalyst, as calculated from the linear fit, was found to be 0.7×10−1 min−1 (Fig. 4d); this value higher is quite comparable with most efficient iron oxide based catalysts developed up to now (see Table S2 in Supplementary material).

In summary, we report the first example of metallic ion functionalized photoluminescent CDs. Fe(III)-containing CDs were prepared via oxidative coupling of xylene by FeCl3 under sonication. The metal ions on the surface make them active catalysts for heterogeneous hydrogenation of various olefins using hydrazine as the hydrogen source. A variety of non-functionalized and functionalized olefins have been reduced in excellent yields and high selectivity; stability and reusability of new catalysts is monitored by photoluminescence intensity measurements. Moreover, the Fe-CDs can be used as highly efficient catalysts for hydrogen peroxide decomposition. We strongly believe that such a unique interplay of redox property still remained unprecedented and can potentially offer significant improvement not only in developing multicatalytic systems, but also opening doors for real time monitoring in range of applications involving other processes of catalysis, biocatalysis, and biomedicine.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic (LO1305 and CZ.1.05/2.1.00/19.0377), the assistance provided by the Research Infrastructure NanoEnviCz supported by the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic under project no. LM2015073 as well as Award No. KUS-C1-018-02 made by King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST). This work was further supported by Student Project IGA_PrF_2017_025 of Palacký University. The authors also acknowledge the help of Dr. Ondrej Malina, Dr. Martin Petr, Dr. Radka Krikavova and Dr. Daikopoulos with material characterization of the samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.apmt.2017.03.002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots—emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics
Broad family of carbon nanoallotropes: classification, chemistry, and applications of fullerenes, carbon dots, nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, and combined superstructures
Quaternized carbon dot-modified graphene oxide for selective cell labelling – controlled nucleus and cytoplasm imaging
Effect of injection routes on the biodistribution, clearance, and tumor uptake of carbon dots
Gd(III)-doped carbon dots as a dual fluorescent-MRI probe
Color-switchable electroluminescence of carbon dot light-emitting diodes
Synergistic antibacterial activity of PEGylated silver–graphene quantum dots nanocomposites
Fluorescent carbon nanodots for targeted in vitro cancer cell imaging
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Coal as an abundant source of graphene quantum dots
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Green and simple route toward boron doped carbon dots with significantly enhanced non-linear optical properties
Photoluminescence effects of graphitic core size and surface functional groups in carbon dots: COO− induced red-shift emission
Carboxylic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform, for DNA detection
Synthesis of strongly fluorescent graphene quantum dots by cage-opening buckminsterfullerene
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots by photochemical stitching of small aromatic molecules: fluorescent nanoprobes in cellular imaging
Iron(III) chloride in oxidative C–C coupling reactions
Oxidative coupling reactions of phenols with FeCl3 in the solid state
Mol. Cryst. Liq. Cryst. Incorporating Nonlinear Opt.
Oxygen insertion catalysis by sp2 carbon
Graphene oxide: a convenient carbocatalyst for facilitating oxidation and hydration reactions
Functionalization of graphene: covalent and non-covalent approaches, derivatives and applications
Iron oxide nanoparticles modified with carbon quantum nanodots for the stabilization of palladium nanoparticles: an efficient catalyst for the suzuki reaction in aqueous media under mild conditions
Solar hydrogen production using carbon quantum dots and a molecular nickel catalyst
Sulfated carbon quantum dots as efficient visible-light switchable acid catalysts for room-temperature ring-opening reactions
Carbon quantum dot sensitized TiO2 nanotube arrays for photoelectrochemical hydrogen generation under visible light
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Catalytic homogeneous asymmetric hydrogenations of largely unfunctionalized alkenes
Hydrogenation of carbon–carbon multiple bonds: chemo-, regio- and stereo-selectivity
Iron-catalysed, general and operationally simple formal hydrogenation using Fe(OTf)3 and NaBH4
Abundant metals give precious hydrogenation performance
Nano-magnetite (Fe3O4) as a support for recyclable catalysts in the development of sustainable methodologies
Clays and oxide minerals as catalysts and nanocatalysts in Fenton-like reactions — a review
Catalytic decomposition of hydrogen peroxide on iron oxide: kinetics, mechanism, and implications
A review of classic Fenton's peroxidation as an advanced oxidation technique
Comparison of oxidative aromatic coupling and the scholl reaction
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
Preparation of carbon nanodots from single chain polymeric nanoparticles and theoretical investigation of the photoluminescence mechanism
Formation mechanism of carbogenic nanoparticles with dual photoluminescence emission
Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials: “quantum dots” or nanoclusters?
Prussian blue modified iron oxide magnetic nanoparticles and their high peroxidase-like activity
Coal as an abundant source of graphene quantum dots
